title: Go-to-Market Strategy
description: Develop a complete go-to-market strategy for a new product launch. This is the most complex project — synthesising every skill you've learned into a launch plan that could work in the real world.
whyItMatters: GTM strategy is where strategy meets execution at the highest level. It's the skill that differentiates marketing managers from marketing leaders. Every successful product launch — from Airbnb to Canva — started with a GTM plan.
prerequisites: Complete the Full Marketing Plan project

steps.title: Define the product
steps.description: Choose a product to launch (real or fictional). Define what it does, what problem it solves, and why it's different. If fictional, make it realistic — something that could actually exist.

steps.title: Market analysis
steps.description: Size the market (TAM, SAM, SOM). Analyse competitors using Porter's Five Forces. Identify the market gap your product fills. Conduct PESTEL analysis for external factors.

steps.title: Customer definition
steps.description: Build detailed personas for your launch audience. Define early adopters vs. mainstream customers. Map the customer journey from discovery to advocacy.

steps.title: Positioning and messaging
steps.description: Complete a brand positioning canvas. Write your positioning statement. Create a messaging framework: tagline, elevator pitch, value propositions for each persona, and key proof points.

steps.title: Pricing strategy
steps.description: Define your pricing model (one-time, subscription, freemium, tiered). Justify with competitive pricing analysis and willingness-to-pay research. Show how pricing supports positioning.
steps.tips: Pricing is the most powerful marketing lever. It communicates value more than any ad.

steps.title: Channel strategy
steps.description: Define your marketing channels: owned (website, email, social), earned (PR, word-of-mouth, SEO), and paid (ads, sponsorships, partnerships). Allocate budget by channel with expected ROI.

steps.title: Launch plan
steps.description: Create a detailed launch timeline: pre-launch (6 weeks), launch week, and post-launch (4 weeks). Include specific activities, responsible parties, and milestones for each phase.

steps.title: Metrics and success criteria
steps.description: Define launch success: what does a successful launch look like at 30, 60, and 90 days? Set specific targets for acquisition, activation, and revenue.

steps.title: Risk assessment
steps.description: Identify 5 things that could go wrong and your mitigation plan for each. This shows senior-level thinking.

rubric.description: Analysis demonstrates genuine understanding of the market, not surface-level research.
Product, positioning, pricing, and channels all reinforce each other.
The plan is detailed enough to actually execute — not just high-level strategy.
Budget is justified, ROI expectations are realistic, and pricing is strategic.
The strategy could be presented to a CEO or investor and hold up to scrutiny.
